target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts, `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every boot and every QMD sync interval walked every session JSONL and called `buildSessionEntry()` per file, which performs full read + parse + redaction + hash/render work even when the transcript export was unchanged.
- **Why it matters:** The previous in-memory skip state only avoided some markdown rewrites after a manager instance had already rebuilt each entry. It did not survive gateway restart, so cold starts and periodic syncs could still create large CPU and GC spikes on production-sized transcript sets.
- **What changed:**
  1. Added a per-agent SQLite cache table, `qmd_session_export_cache`, in `openclaw-agent.sqlite`, keyed by `(session_file, export_dir, render_version)`.
  2. Added a stat + full-content-fingerprint fast path before `buildSessionEntry()`. When cached `size`, `mtimeMs`, `ino`, full content fingerprint, and cached markdown target all match, QMD skips the expensive full-session rebuild.
  3. Preserved the slow path for changed transcripts, missing markdown targets, mismatched cached targets, retention pruning, export-dir changes, and render-version changes.
  4. Kept raw per-agent DB/Kysely access inside the core state owner and exposed only QMD-specific cache helpers through a private local-only SDK subpath, `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal`, scoped to the bundled `memory-core` plugin.
  5. Batch-loaded the current export scope cache rows once per `exportSessions()` pass and reused an in-memory lookup map, so large transcript sets do not reopen/refresh the per-agent DB accessor once per session file.
  6. Excluded the generated private QMD SDK helper artifacts from npm package output: `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.js` and `!dist/plugin-sdk/memory-core-qmd-export-cache-internal.d.ts`.

**Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown()`, session redaction, line wrapping, and the markdown format QMD consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry()` remains the authoritative slow path. The cache is scoped by export directory and `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION`, so config moves and render-byte changes invalidate cleanly.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB) — prior-head context, not current-head proof:**
- Boot QMD sync: **312s -> single-digit seconds**
- Interval QMD sync: **38s -> ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

> ⚠️ **Benchmark provenance:** these numbers were measured on an early head of
> this branch and were **not** re-run after the SQLite-backed cache rewrite,
> the private-SDK boundary work, the full-content-hash fast path, the
> target-byte fingerprint validation, or the latest `origin/main` merge. They
> remain a faithful description of the *mechanism's* impact (eliminating the
> per-file `buildSessionEntry()` rebuild on unchanged transcripts) but should
> be treated as **observational context, not current-head benchmark proof**.
> Re-running them requires Eddie's live 4,052-session production corpus on the
> running gateway, which is not reproducible from an agent worktree. The
> `## Real Behavior Proof` section below is the authoritative current-head
> behavioral evidence.

## Linked Issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test Coverage

New and updated tests cover the cache contract:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
  - persists session-export state across manager restarts
  - rebuilds after same-size transcript rewrites, proving content changes are not hidden by matching stat fields
  - ignores cache rows scoped to a previous `exportDir`
  - rebuilds markdown when the cached target has been deleted
  - rebuilds the expected markdown when a current-scope cache row points at another target
  - preserves existing session-export behavior for checkpoint/session filtering
- `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.test.ts`
  - schema shape includes the new derived-state cache table
  - per-agent DB schema metadata and `user_version` remain at version 1
  - newer-schema refusal still protects downgrade safety
- `test/release-check.test.ts`
  - package output rejects private local-only plugin SDK artifacts
  - package `files` exclusions include every private local-only SDK artifact, including the new QMD cache helper

## Tradeoffs / Known Caveats

- The fast path still uses stat identity to avoid hashing obviously changed files, but it verifies a full SHA-1 content fingerprint before skipping the rebuild. Same-size rewrites with preserved or seeded stat identity do not suppress updated markdown.
- `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` must be bumped when changing `renderSessionMarkdown()`, redaction, line wrapping, or any other logic that changes the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`.
- The cache table is derived state; the per-agent DB `user_version` remains at 1 for rollback safety.
- No hand-written `CHANGELOG.md` entry is included; current repo policy keeps release notes out of normal PRs and derives them during release generation.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior addressed:** QMD session export persists its unchanged-transcript fast-path state in the per-agent SQLite DB without exposing raw per-agent DB/Kysely access through the public plugin SDK. It must still rebuild markdown when a cached target is missing, when the cached target points elsewhere, when stat identity matches but the transcript content fingerprint changed, **and when the export target's own bytes were externally modified/corrupted while the source transcript stayed unchanged** (target-byte fingerprint validation; self-heals on the next sync). The private QMD SDK artifact must also stay out of package output.

- **Canonical current-head proof script:** `scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts` (run with `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts`). It drives the **real private `QmdMemoryManager.exportSessions()` method** — not a re-implementation — on a real `QmdMemoryManager.create({ mode: "status" })` instance against real per-agent SQLite + the real session-transcript corpus accessor + real fs (no Vitest, no mocked cache seam). `mode: "status"` initializes collections without spawning the `qmd` binary, and `exportSessions()` shells out to nothing, so the entire seam (stat fast path, source content fingerprint, target-byte fingerprint, slow-path rebuild, cache upsert, stale-entry deletion, artifact-mapping write) runs as shipped. Scenarios: (a) cold cache → builds + persists a non-null target fingerprint (asserted == SHA-1 of the written bytes); (b) clean hit → skip with no rewrite (target mtime preserved); (c) corrupted target bytes → rebuild + self-heal (the pinned regression); (d) source changed → rebuild with new content; (e.1) legacy NULL `target_fingerprint` + correct bytes → repopulate the fingerprint, then verified skip; (e.2) legacy NULL + corrupted target → rebuild. Output ends `All runtime assertions passed.` Focused vitest regressions in `qmd-manager.test.ts` pin the same paths in the unit suite.
- **Real environment tested:** Current PR head `3c923479bc` in the `perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` worktree, using the real `QmdMemoryManager`, real `resolveMemoryBackendConfig`, the real `listSessionTranscriptCorpusEntriesForAgent` accessor (resolved through the real runtime-config snapshot), the private local-only QMD cache subpath, and real per-agent `openclaw-agent.sqlite` storage. The proof ran against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`; no Vitest harness or mocked QMD export cache was used, and the `qmd` binary was never invoked (it is not on the export path).
- **Exact steps run:** `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts` from the topic worktree. The script seeds one session JSONL + a `sessions.json` identity, then calls the real `exportSessions()` repeatedly while mutating the source transcript, the on-disk markdown target, and the cache row's `target_fingerprint` to exercise each scenario; every SKIP-vs-REBUILD decision is made by the shipped method.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the live run (also saved under `~/reports/proof-77158/run-output.txt`):

```text
[a] cold export -> built target and persisted target fingerprint
[b] clean cache hit -> skipped rebuild (target untouched)
[c] corrupted target bytes -> rebuilt and self-healed (regression pinned)
[d] source changed -> rebuilt with new content
[e.1] legacy NULL fingerprint + correct bytes -> repopulated, then verified skip
[e.2] legacy NULL fingerprint + corrupted target -> rebuilt (corruption healed)
All runtime assertions passed.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** (a) the cold export built the markdown and persisted a non-null target fingerprint equal to the SHA-1 of the written bytes; (b) the clean hit left the target's mtime and content untouched, proving the verified fast path; (c) a corrupted target was rebuilt and self-healed, with the next round skipping again; (d) a changed source rebuilt with the new content; (e.1) a legacy NULL-fingerprint row (correct bytes) re-entered the slow path and repopulated the fingerprint, then the next round was a verified skip; (e.2) a legacy NULL-fingerprint row over a corrupted target rebuilt rather than trusting the unverifiable bytes. Final cache state tracks exactly the one exported session.
- **What was not tested:** I did not rerun a benchmark against Eddie's full 4,052-session production corpus after the SDK-boundary rewrite, base-branch merge, package exclusion fix, cached-target validation fix, target-byte fingerprint validation, and batch-read follow-up. That corpus lives on Eddie's running gateway and is not reproducible from an agent worktree. The proof above (and `scripts/proof-77158-qmd-export-target-validation.ts`) validates the current SQLite cache behavior and all regression paths on real runtime code; the original production-sized timing numbers remain observational context, not current-head benchmark proof (see the ⚠️ caveat under Summary).

## Verification

Current head `3c923479bc` includes:

- `3c923479bc` (`perf(qmd): real exportSessions() integration proof; drop changelog edit (review feedback)`) — the latest review round: (1) the proof script now drives the **real private `exportSessions()`** method end to end (see `## Real Behavior Proof` above) instead of re-implementing the cache decision inline; (2) the release-owned `CHANGELOG.md` `## Unreleased` block is removed (restored byte-identical to `main`).
- `2d3cc42a55` (`perf(qmd): validate cached export target bytes before skip (review feedback)`) — the [P2] target-byte fingerprint validation fix: the fast path now reads the export target and compares its SHA-1 against the cached rendered-markdown fingerprint before skipping; on drift (or a legacy NULL fingerprint) it falls through to the slow path, which self-heals the corrupted target. Adds a nullable `target_fingerprint` column via additive `ALTER` (no schema-version bump).
- `33a9ac2566` (`chore(qmd): refresh plugin SDK API baseline after main merge (review feedback)`) — regenerated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` (tracked file only; `.json`/`.jsonl` are gitignored) after the latest `origin/main` merge shifted the generated SDK surface (corpus refactor #96162 and adjacent memory-host-sdk work). Deterministic across repeated `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen` runs.
- `3468c322a6` (`fix(qmd): exclude private cache SDK artifact from package`)
- signed base-refresh merges from current `openclaw/main` to clear the PR's dirty/conflicting merge state
- cleanup restoring unrelated Slack/Telegram/installer/test formatter churn back to current `openclaw/main`, leaving exactly the QMD/cache/SDK/package diff

**Corpus-entry preservation ([P2] #2 in the latest Codex review): verified, no change needed.** Current head's `exportSessions()` already iterates `listSessionTranscriptCorpusEntriesForAgent(this.agentId)` and threads each `corpusEntry` into both `buildSessionEntry()` (`generatedByDreamingNarrative` / `generatedByCronRun`) and `buildSessionArtifactMapping()` — in the fast path AND the slow path — then calls `replaceQmdSessionArtifactMappings(...)`. The `buildSessionArtifactMapping` body is byte-identical to `origin/main`. The accessor-backed corpus identity flow from #96162 is fully preserved; the cache layer short-circuits only the expensive rebuild, never the corpus identity mapping.

**Schema-version-1 derived-cache table — maintainer acceptance item (not a code bug):** the new per-agent `qmd_session_export_cache` table (and its additive `target_fingerprint` column) intentionally keeps `OPENCLAW_AGENT_SCHEMA_VERSION = 1` because it is disposable derived state — a cold/older build simply rebuilds the cache; there is no migration to roll back. This is a deliberate rollback-safety decision that needs a maintainer's explicit acceptance, not a version bump. Flagged for maintainer judgment rather than changed unilaterally.

**Prior art for the version-1 additive-table strategy:** every table ever added to `openclaw-agent.sqlite` has followed the same convention — `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` at `user_version = 1`, no version bump, no migration required. The full history on `origin/main`:

| PR / commit | Tables added | Schema version after |
|---|---|---|
| `#88349` (Add per-agent SQLite cache store) | `schema_meta`, `cache_entries` | 1 |
| `#89102` (store auth profiles in sqlite) | `auth_profile_store`, `auth_profile_state` | 1 |
| memory index PRs | `memory_index_meta`, `memory_index_sources`, `memory_index_chunks`, `memory_embedding_cache`, `memory_index_state` | 1 |
| **this PR** | `qmd_session_export_cache` | **1** |

The only time a version bump was attempted (`fix/heartbeat-claude-auth-schema-v2`, a compat shim for v2-stamped agent DBs) it was reverted. The project has consistently treated additive derived-state tables as rollback-safe at version 1; a version bump is reserved for changes that require forward-only migration and must block rollback. `qmd_session_export_cache` contains no user data and can be silently orphaned by a downgrade with no behavioral consequence, which is exactly the class of change this strategy is designed for.

**Base status (GitHub `mergeable: CONFLICTING`):** the branch already contains all of the fork's `main`; the conflict is pure base drift — upstream `main` is currently a few hours / ~31 commits ahead of the fork `main` this branch is built from. A trial merge of current upstream `main` produced exactly one auto-resolvable conflict (the generated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256`), with `qmd-manager.ts`, its test, and `package.json` all auto-merging cleanly — so this is a clean base-advance that clears once the routine fork sync lands and the baseline is regenerated. No substantive code conflict exists.

target.title: feat(tools/write): add append mode for agent writes
target.body:
## Summary

Revives and rebases the closed append-mode work from #75549 for the canonical data-loss issue #40001.

The `write` tool currently overwrites existing files. OpenClaw has an append-only wrapper for memory-flush runs, but ordinary agent turns, cron skills, and monitor-and-react workflows do not have a first-class append path. This PR adds a backwards-compatible optional `append: true` parameter to `write` so agents can append without synthesizing a read-concatenate-overwrite sequence.

When `append` is omitted or false, existing overwrite behavior is unchanged.

## What changed

- Adds optional `append?: boolean` handling for host/workspace and sandbox write operations.
- Preserves existing workspace/root guard behavior.
- Serializes concurrent appends through the existing file mutation queue.
- Preserves validation behavior for blank paths and invalid content.
- Surfaces sandbox I/O failures except for missing-file cases used to create-on-append.
- Honors aborted append calls before entering the mutation queue.
- Adds focused regression coverage for append-create, append-accumulate, append-false-overwrites, validation, and sandbox/workspace behavior.

## Validation

Run in an isolated checkout on current `origin/main`:

- `corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.params.test.ts`
  - passed: `10 files / 170 tests`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm lint:core`
  - passed: `0 warnings / 0 errors`

## Notes

This PR intentionally keeps the API small and backwards-compatible. It does not remove or relax the existing memory-flush append-only wrapper; it gives ordinary `write` callers an explicit append path instead of relying on prompt discipline or destructive read/concatenate/write patterns.

Fixes #40001.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** The agent write tool supports `append: true` without overwriting the existing file, while still using the workspace-root guard for workspace-only host writes.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux using the actual `createHostWorkspaceWriteTool` implementation against a temporary workspace on the real filesystem.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx` terminal smoke that created the host workspace write tool, wrote `first line`, reran the same write tool with `append: true` for `second line`, then read the resulting file.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the smoke:

  ```text
  workspace: $TMPDIR/openclaw-write-append-proof-.../workspace
  tool result: append:true preserved existing content and added the second line
  file content:
  first line
  second line
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The final file retained the original content and appended the new content in order; the append path did not truncate or replace the file.
- **What was not tested:** No live Gateway session was exercised; validation used the real tool implementation in an isolated local checkout plus targeted regression/type checks.

## Summary

- preserve the newer Codex wrapper diagnostics, safe isolated config copying, process cleanup behavior, and API-key auth materialization from current `main`
- document the override in the ACP router skill and plugin manifest

## Latest update
- rebased onto current `main` at `4ecb45bf77`
- removed the normal-PR `CHANGELOG.md` entry
- added test isolation so host `CODEX_HOME` / `OPENCLAW_ACPX_CODEX_HOME` environment values do not make wrapper tests depend on the developer machine

## Validation
- `pnpm test extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/codex-auth-bridge.test.ts` - 2 files / 36 tests passed
- `pnpm test:extension acpx` - 13 files / 145 tests passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/acpx/src/config-schema.ts extensions/acpx/src/config.ts extensions/acpx/src/codex-auth-bridge.ts extensions/acpx/src/codex-auth-bridge.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/config.test.ts extensions/acpx/openclaw.plugin.json extensions/acpx/skills/acp-router/SKILL.md`

isolated_default {"codexHomeMode":"wrapper-local","configExists":true,"command":"\"/usr/bin/node\" \"<wrapper>/codex-acp-wrapper.mjs\""}

target.title: fix(cli): exit after hooks inspection output
target.body:
## Summary

- port #70255 onto current main
- keep hooks list/info/check/enable/disable and bare `hooks` as one-shot CLI commands
- keep relay plus deprecated install/update behavior unchanged
- add the missing changelog entry

## Why

Plugin diagnostics can load third-party hook plugins. If a plugin leaves a timer or other handle open, the hooks CLI can print output but keep the process alive. These hooks commands are inspection or one-shot mutation commands, so they should exit after successful output.

Supersedes #70255.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts src/plugins/status.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/cli/hooks-cli.ts src/cli/hooks-cli.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: One-shot `openclaw hooks list --json` should print its output and exit instead of hanging after hook/plugin diagnostics leave open handles.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and the real CLI path, not a unit test.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the actual CLI command with a 20 second watchdog from the PR branch: `pnpm openclaw hooks list --json`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text
$ python3 watchdog-wrapper-for pnpm openclaw hooks list --json
exit:0
stdout_bytes:273
stdout_head:
> openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/doruk/Desktop/Asuman/.worktrees/openclaw-hooks-cli-exit-refresh
> node scripts/run-node.mjs hooks list --json

{
  "workspaceDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/workspace",
  "managedHooksDir": "/Users/doruk/.openclaw/hooks",
  "hooks": []
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The command exited with status 0 before the 20 second watchdog fired and returned the hooks JSON output.
- What was not tested: No separate hanging third-party plugin was installed for this proof. The focused regression test still covers the lingering-handle path.

target.title: fix(discord): preserve target intent during normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Discord-like explicit DM targets could be normalized through the id-like target path without preserving the intended direct-message kind.
- Why it matters: A Discord target that is syntactically a user/direct target should stay routed as a user/direct target instead of falling back to generic group/channel inference.
- What changed: Shared target-kind inference now lives in `target-normalization`, and both outbound target resolution and cron delivery pass the inferred kind into id-like plugin resolution.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No Discord runtime sending behavior, config schema, plugin manifest, or user-facing docs changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

target.title: fix(plugins): supplement ClawHub artifact metadata
target.body:
## Summary

- Accept current ClawHub artifact resolver responses where `artifact` is the package artifact summary shape.
- When the resolver response has a legacy zip artifact but no archive verification metadata, load the version metadata and use its `sha256hash` or `files[]` for verification.
- Add a regression test for the current `clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9` response shape.

## Why

The ClawHub artifact endpoint can return a legacy zip artifact summary while the install verification metadata is only present on the version endpoint. OpenClaw rejected that package as missing verification metadata, even though the package was valid and installable.

## Tests

- `pnpm test src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.ts src/plugins/clawhub.test.ts && git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Real-world validation

- `HOME=$tmp/home OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=$tmp/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=$tmp/openclaw.json pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi@3.8.9 --dangerously-force-unsafe-install`
- Result: installed `zeroapi-router` from ClawHub in an isolated temp state.

## AI-assisted

Yes. I used Codex and reviewed the changed code and tests.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Installing a ClawHub legacy zip plugin must verify archive metadata using the full version metadata path when the artifact resolver only returns summary metadata.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, using this PR branch and an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`, installing the live `clawhub:zeroapi` package.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the real CLI install path from the PR branch: `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l pnpm openclaw plugins install clawhub:zeroapi --force`.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the real CLI command:

```text
OPENCLAW_HOME:/tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l
exit:0
> openclaw@2026.5.4 openclaw /Users/doruk/Desktop/Asuman/.worktrees/openclaw-clawhub-artifact-metadata
> node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install clawhub:zeroapi --force

Resolving clawhub:zeroapi...
ClawHub code-plugin zeroapi@3.8.12 channel=community verification=source-linked
Compatibility: pluginApi=>=2026.5.2 minGateway=2026.5.2
ClawHub package "zeroapi" is community...
Downloading plugin zeroapi@3.8.12 from ClawHub...
Extracting ... zeroapi-3.8.12.tgz...
Plugin manifest id "zeroapi-router" differs from npm package name "zeroapi"; using manifest id as the config key.
Installing to /tmp/openclaw-real-proof-NSAg8l/.openclaw/extensions/zeroapi-router...
Installed plugin: zeroapi-router
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
stderr_tail: [plugins] zeroapi-config.json not found. Run /zeroapi to configure.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live ClawHub plugin install resolved version metadata, downloaded and extracted the plugin, and exited with status 0 in a fresh temporary OpenClaw home.
- What was not tested: I did not restart a full gateway from the temporary install. The proof covers the resolver/install path changed by this PR, while focused tests cover metadata fallback behavior.

target.title: fix: prune all history images when current turn has fresh attachments
target.body:
## Problem

When sending a new image for analysis, stale image data from previous conversation turns can contaminate the model's interpretation of the current image. Reported in #66702.

## Solution

- Add `HistoryImagePruneOptions` interface with `preserveRecentCompletedTurns` parameter
- Thread option through `resolvePruneBeforeIndex` → `pruneProcessedHistoryImages` → caller in `attempt.ts`
- When current turn has fresh images, pass `preserveRecentCompletedTurns: 0` to strip all historical image data
- No new images: existing conservative default (preserve 3 turns) applies

Backward-compatible. Existing tests unchanged.

Fixes #66702

target.title: fix(memory, builtin backend): eagerly preload session transcript listeners at gateway startup
target.body:
## Summary (scope narrowed to builtin backend per Codex review)

Eagerly preload `MemoryIndexManager` at gateway startup for every agent whose `agents.defaults.memorySearch.sources` (or per-agent overlay) includes `"sessions"`, **when using the builtin session-file backend**. This arms `ensureSessionListener()` before the first `sessionTranscriptUpdate` event fires, so `/reset` and `/new` archive emits actually land in the listener set and get indexed into `chunks` without the user having to run `openclaw memory index --force`.

## Why it was broken

`MemoryIndexManager` is lazy-loaded on the first `memory_search` tool call. If a user types `/reset` or `/new` before that first search, the builtin `archiveFileOnDisk` emit lands in an empty `SESSION_TRANSCRIPT_LISTENERS` set and is silently dropped, so the resulting `.jsonl.reset.<iso>` / `.jsonl.deleted.<iso>` archives never enter `chunks`. Only `memory index --force` recovers them (which is #76311 / #76452's escape hatch but is not a cold-start fix).

## Why builtin-only (Codex review response)

Thanks @clawsweeper for the P2 catch on the qmd path. The initial revision of this PR treated any successful `getActiveMemorySearchManager` result as listener-armed, but under the qmd backend that call returns the `FallbackMemoryManager` wrapper whose inner `MemoryIndexManager` is only constructed by `fallbackFactory`. So on qmd, merely obtaining the wrapper does NOT run `ensureSessionListener()`, and the cold-start race still drops `sessionTranscriptUpdate` emits.

Three options were on the table (per Codex):
1. Explicitly preload the builtin `MemoryIndexManager` listener owner even for qmd — requires reaching into the FallbackMemoryManager fallback slot.
2. Add qmd-native transcript event handling inside `qmd-manager.ts`.
3. **Narrow this PR to builtin-only and defer qmd to a follow-up.**

This revision takes **option 3**: added an explicit early-return guard `if (params.cfg.memory?.backend === "qmd") { ... return; }` at the top of `startGatewayMemorySessionListeners`, with a log line making the scope decision visible, plus a regression test (`skips preload entirely when memory.backend is qmd (Codex review scope guard)`). The changelog entry is also retitled `Memory/sessions (builtin backend):` and calls out the intentional qmd deferral.

## Independent verification (builtin only)

CarHer builtin-backend deployment (`carher-199` baseline). Three cold restarts, same container, identical `/reset` sequence:

| | pre-preload | post-preload |
|---|---|---|
| archive emit lands in listener set | ❌ dropped | ✅ captured |
| `chunks` delta after 30s | +0 | +41 |
| verified commit | `main @ 9a49d143c819` | `HEAD of this PR` |

## What is NOT included

- qmd backend session-listener arming (follow-up PR, tracked in body + changelog).
- Changes to `qmd-manager.ts` transcript event handling.
- Changes to `FallbackMemoryManager` fallback construction.

Refs #56131. AI-assisted.

target.title: fix(ui): make dreams diary day chips sticky to prevent scroll-away on long entries
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #76638 — when a diary entry had long content (10+ paragraphs), the day-chip navigation bar at the top would be pushed out of the viewport, making it impossible to switch between dates.

## Root cause

`.dreams-diary__daychips` used `position: relative` with no sticky behavior. Long `<article>` content would scroll the entire area, pushing the chips off-screen.

## Fix

Changed `.dreams-diary__daychips`:
- `position: relative` → `position: sticky; top: 0`
- Added `background: var(--bg)` to prevent content bleed-through
- Bumped `z-index` from 1 to 2 to stay above diary entries

## Test plan

- [x] Verified CSS parses without errors
- [x] Day chips remain visible when scrolling long diary entries

target.title: docs(skills/coding-agent): worktree-isolate long spawns + watchdog-trap mitigations
target.body:
## Summary

Adds operator-facing guidance to defuse a recurring failure mode in
multi-agent orchestration: a watchdog-reported "terminated" spawn
that actually kept running and silently raced the parent agent on a
shared working tree.

We hit this in real downstream work — the parent agent received a
harness \`produced no output for 180s and was terminated\` message,
treated the spawn slot as free, started doing per-file integration
work in the same project root, and then spent ~30 minutes chasing
phantom regressions before checking \`pgrep\` and discovering the
"killed" worker was still alive and overwriting staged changes.

This PR is the operator-side workaround. A complementary runtime
change to relabel the watchdog message itself (something like
"watchdog tripped — process may still be running; verify with \`ps -p
<PID>\`") would fix the root trap; the skill changes here are the
documented mitigations until that ships.

## Changes (all in \`skills/coding-agent/SKILL.md\`)

1. **Claude Code section** now distinguishes foreground \`--print\`
   (short tasks) from background long-running, where
   \`--output-format stream-json\` is preferred so the harness watchdog
   sees continuous liveness instead of silently ticking down on
   \`--print\`'s buffered output.

2. **New section: "Verifying a spawn is actually dead"** explains
   that the watchdog message is advisory, lists the working-tree
   symptoms of a rogue spawn racing the parent (files reverting,
   phantom untracked files, edits disappearing, unrelated test
   failures), and prescribes \`pgrep <agent-binary>\` as the first
   diagnostic step.

3. **Progress Updates section** gets one new bullet: don't treat a
   harness "terminated" message as a confirmed kill; verify before
   spawning a replacement. Points readers at the new section.

4. **Two new Rules entries** codify the above as hard rules:
   - **#12** — Worktree-isolate any long-running spawn that would
     otherwise share a working tree with the parent agent.
   - **#13** — Watchdog "terminated" messages are advisory, not
     authoritative. Verify with \`ps -p\` / \`pgrep\`. If \`--print\`-mode
     Claude Code spawns repeatedly trip the watchdog, switch to
     \`--output-format stream-json\`.

## Why these specifically

- \`--output-format stream-json\` for long backgrounded Claude Code is
  the prevention; \`pgrep + worktree isolation\` is the cure. Both belong
  in this skill because both are operator-facing CLI patterns.
- The new section sits between Progress Updates and the Rules block
  because it's a diagnostic playbook, not a rule.
- The Rules block already has 11 entries; #12 and #13 extend the
  existing \`~/Projects/openclaw/\` and \`~/.openclaw/\` "blast-radius"
  rules with the analogous "any project working tree the parent
  agent is using" rule.

## Test plan

- [ ] Read the diff (62 lines added, 1 deleted; one file).
- [ ] Confirm the new patterns don't conflict with existing skills
      anywhere else in the registry.
- [ ] If the runtime team wants to relabel the watchdog message at
      the source, this PR can land first as a workaround and that
      change can supersede the diagnostic section later.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

> - Claim items by adding your GitHub handle to the `Assignee` column
> - Priority guide: **P0** = crash/blocker/security, **P1** = significant bug/regression, **P2** = minor bug/enhancement, **P3** = nice-to-have/stale

target.title: fix(agent): route explicit channel targets per recipient

## Summary
- Fixes #41483 by deriving per-recipient agent session keys for `openclaw agent --agent ... --channel ... --to ...` instead of falling back to `agent:<id>:main`.
- Preserves the existing #60614 behavior where `--session-id` takes precedence over the implicit `--agent` main-session fallback.
- Carries forward and credits the focused approach from @pingfanfan in source PR https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/41557, while addressing the review blockers raised there.

## Review notes
- Keep explicit `--session-key` authoritative over `--session-id` lookup.
- Preserve global-session continuity for non-deliverable-channel paths.
- Ensure cross-store session-id scans skip the already-searched primary agent store, not an unrelated default store.

## Validation
- `pnpm check:changed`

target.title: fix(ios): stabilize chat streaming layout and session flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: iOS chat streaming could attach to a synthetic placeholder lane, churn during initial history/bootstrap, and reparse streaming content in ways that caused flicker or session drift.
- Why it matters: active conversations can show unstable streaming UI, lose the correct lane/session association, or visibly flicker while tokens arrive.
- What changed: scoped streaming to the active session key, hardened stream acceptance/initial load behavior, and stabilized layout parsing/visibility updates.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway protocol changes, no new model behavior. This fix lives in the shared `OpenClawChatUI` layer used by both iOS and macOS. The bug (cross-run UI drift during concurrent session-key agent events) affects any client using `ChatViewModel`; macOS uses the same chat view and benefits equally, but the fix is not platform-specific — it guards shared streaming state.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: targeted shared-package tests plus iOS build validation.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #40985
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: streaming state was not consistently tied to the active session key, and downstream UI/layout updates re-parsed or re-evaluated visibility in ways that amplified bootstrap churn into visible instability.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was insufficient coverage around session-scoped streaming updates plus layout stability under rapid token updates/history refresh.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): this branch supersedes #40985 by fixing the broader lane/acceptance/layout causes instead of only the earlier visible symptom.
- Why this regressed now: incremental fixes addressed isolated symptoms while the newer streaming lane introduced more state transitions that needed session-scoped and layout-stable handling.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: the issue was not isolated to one text-rendering widget; it involved session scoping, bootstrap timing, and layout parsing together.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/ChatViewModelTests.swift`
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/AssistantTextParserTests.swift`
- Scenario the test should lock in: session-scoped streaming updates must survive bootstrap/history churn and preserve stable parsed output/visibility while tokens stream.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in shared chat state/parsing logic, so package-level tests cover the core behavior without requiring a full end-to-end harness.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): targeted parser/view-model tests were added/expanded for this branch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- iOS chat streaming stays attached to the active session instead of a synthetic placeholder lane.
- Streaming text/layout behaves more stably during initial load/history refresh.
- Visible flicker/churn from repeated parse/visibility mismatches is reduced.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local dev environment
- Runtime/container: shared OpenClawKit package + iOS app build
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): iOS chat UI / streaming flow
- Relevant config (redacted): local iOS simulator build

### Steps

1. Build the shared package and iOS app from this branch.
2. Open a chat session and trigger a streaming reply.
3. Observe initial history/bootstrap plus active token streaming.

### Expected

- Streaming binds to the active session.
- Bootstrap/history churn does not destabilize the active stream.
- Layout remains visually stable while tokens arrive.

### Actual

- Matched expected in targeted tests and local build validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests`
  - `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter AssistantTextParserTests`
  - `xcodegen generate`
  - `xcodebuild -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClaw -destination 'generic/platform=iOS Simulator' CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO build`
- Edge cases checked:
  - stream acceptance during bootstrap/history load
  - parser stability under repeated streaming updates
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full repository CI matrix
  - non-iOS clients

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
  - All 19 review threads (P1/P2 from `chatgpt-codex-connector[bot]` + `copilot-pull-request-reviewer`) resolved in this update.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A



## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: chat-state timing bugs can reappear under bootstrapping or external-final sync edge cases.
  - Mitigation: this patch scopes state to the active session and adds targeted parser/view-model coverage around the churn paths.



## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: iOS/shared OpenClawChatUI streaming state remains scoped to the active session, load recovery remains idempotent after a failed initial bootstrap plus successful manual refresh, and streaming text/tool state does not get cleared by a later view `load()` call.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS local dev environment, Xcode 26.5 SDKs, Swift Testing 1902, OpenClawKit shared package and generated iOS Xcode project from this PR head `591a724df2531d373fc8603f2faa323fc93bc053`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests
swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter AssistantTextParserTests
xcodegen generate --spec apps/ios/project.yml
xcodebuild -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClawChatUI -destination 'generic/platform=iOS Simulator' CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO build
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
ChatViewModelTests: Test run with 83 tests in 1 suite passed after 5.550 seconds.
AssistantTextParserTests: Test run with 7 tests in 1 suite passed after 0.001 seconds.
xcodegen generate --spec apps/ios/project.yml: Created project at /Users/eulices/projects/openclaw-pr50483/apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj
xcodebuild OpenClawChatUI generic iOS Simulator: ** BUILD SUCCEEDED **
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The recovered-load regression is covered by `successful refresh after failed load makes later load idempotent`, which passed after confirming a failed first bootstrap can recover through manual refresh and a later `load()` does not issue another history request or clear the active stream. The shared `OpenClawChatUI` target also built successfully for iOS Simulator from the generated project.
- **What was not tested**: Full `OpenClaw` app scheme build was attempted but blocked by local Xcode environment missing watchOS 26.5 for the embedded Watch app; the directly changed shared chat UI target was built instead. Full repository CI matrix was left to GitHub.

target.title: feat(session): add chat-channel session switching with LRU history retention
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Chat channels did not support listing or switching between prior sessions under the same SessionKey, and follow-up fixes were needed to preserve switchable transcripts until LRU eviction and restore per-session usage/compaction snapshots correctly.
- Why it matters: Without in-chat session switching, users cannot navigate past conversations from Feishu/DingTalk/Telegram/Discord-style chat surfaces; incorrect transcript/archive or snapshot behavior would also make `/session back`, `/sessions`, and `/status`-adjacent state feel unreliable.
- What changed: Added `/sessions`, `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for chat channels; introduced per-SessionKey LRU `sessionHistory`; preserved switchable transcripts until history eviction; restored token/context/compaction snapshots on switch; documented matching/order/retention behavior; cleaned up reset semantics so `session.historyLimit=0` truly disables switchable history.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change SessionKey routing, does not add cross-SessionKey switching, does not add named sessions/UI picker flows, and does not change channel plugin-specific transport behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #9959
- Related #28168

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Added `/sessions` to list switchable sessions for the current chat SessionKey.
- Added `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for in-chat session switching.
- Current session is included in `/sessions`, marked as `[current]`.
- `/sessions` now shows compact single-line preview rows with session ID prefix and truncated preview text.
- Session switching now restores stored token/context/compaction snapshots for the target session.
- `session.historyLimit` now consistently supports `0` as “disable switchable session history”.
- Transcripts for sessions still in history remain switchable; transcripts are archived when evicted from the LRU history queue.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw dev runtime / source tree
- Model/provider: N/A for core session-switch logic
- Integration/channel (if any): chat-channel command flow; manually validated in a real chat session
- Relevant config (redacted):
  - `session.historyLimit: 5` (default path)
  - `session.historyLimit: 0` (disable-history regression path)

### Steps

1. Start a chat conversation and create multiple sessions with `/new`.
2. Run `/sessions`, then switch via `/session <number>` or `/session back`.
3. Continue creating sessions until the history limit is exceeded.

### Expected

- `/sessions` lists current + recent switchable sessions in LRU order.
- Switching restores the target session’s stored metadata/snapshots.
- Sessions still present in history remain switchable.
- The oldest session is archived only after LRU eviction.
- With `session.historyLimit=0`, switchable history is disabled and the previous session is archived immediately on reset.

### Actual

- Behavior matches the expected results above in targeted tests and manual validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `/sessions` output in chat
  - `/session back` restores the previous session
  - switching to a still-retained historical session restores expected context/state
  - LRU eviction archives only the oldest evicted session
  - daily reset path preserves switchable history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` disables switchable history and archives immediately on reset
- Edge cases checked:
  - history boundary behavior at the configured limit
  - selecting current session is a no-op
  - unique-prefix SessionId selection behavior
  - stale/daily-reset session rollover
- What you did **not** verify:
  - every individual chat provider/plugin surface
  - cross-SessionKey switching (out of scope)
  - any UI/card-based session picker (out of scope)

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
  - revert this PR, or set `session.historyLimit=0` to disable switchable session history
- Files/config to restore:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts`
  - `src/config/sessions/types.ts`
  - `src/config/types.base.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - `/sessions` shows stale/incorrect ordering
  - switched sessions lose expected token/context/compaction snapshot state
  - transcripts are archived too early while still present in history
  - `session.historyLimit=0` does not disable switchable history

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: LRU eviction/archive timing could regress and archive a session that should still remain switchable.
  - Mitigation: Added/updated targeted tests covering retained-history vs evicted-history behavior.

- Risk: Snapshot restore could leave token/context/compaction state inconsistent after switching.
  - Mitigation: Added lifecycle tests covering restore and missing-metadata fallback behavior.

- Risk: Docs could drift from implementation again as follow-up fixes land.
  - Mitigation: Updated `docs/design/chat-session-switch.md` to match the current implementation semantics in this PR.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted: Yes

I used an AI assistant for code review, doc cleanup, and targeted test updates. I manually reviewed the final diff, understood the changes, and verified the session-switch flows myself.

target.title: fix(models): mark local Ollama rows available
target.body:
Closes #92224

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users running `openclaw models list --json` could see a reachable local Ollama model reported with `local: true` but `available: false` when registry model-level availability did not include that exact model key.

## Why This Change Was Made

Local model rows already classify loopback/local `baseUrl` values, and the models CLI documents availability/auth as provider-level metadata from local-provider markers rather than a live per-model execution probe. This change treats local provider rows as available from that local marker while preserving registry model-key availability and provider-auth fallback for non-local rows.

## User Impact

Users with local Ollama models now get model-list output that matches the local-provider auth semantics: configured local Ollama rows no longer look unavailable solely because registry discovery omitted the exact model key.

## Evidence

- Claim proved: a reachable local Ollama model is listed with `local: true` and `available: true` on the patched branch.
- Commands / artifacts:
  - `docker run -d --rm --name openclaw-ollama-proof -p 11434:11434 alpine/ollama:latest` -> started a real Ollama daemon reachable at `http://127.0.0.1:11434`.
  - `docker exec openclaw-ollama-proof ollama pull smollm2:135m` -> pulled a local model into that daemon.
  - `curl.exe -sS http://127.0.0.1:11434/api/tags` -> returned `smollm2:135m` from the daemon.
  - `node --import tsx src/entry.ts models list --json --provider ollama` at commit `b5390f5c15`, with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, isolated `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and `OLLAMA_API_KEY=ollama-local` -> passed.
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/models/list.model-row.test.ts --reporter=verbose` -> passed, 4 tests.
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/models/list.rows.test.ts --reporter=verbose` -> passed, 4 tests.
  - `git diff --check` -> passed.
  - `python .agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\autoreview --mode local` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings.
- Observed result: the new regression test fails before the fix with `expected false to be true`, then passes after local base URL rows are included in row availability.
- Real Ollama CLI proof output:

```text
COMMAND: node --import tsx src/entry.ts models list --json --provider ollama
COMMIT: b5390f5c15
OLLAMA_DAEMON: Docker Desktop container openclaw-ollama-proof, alpine/ollama:latest, reachable at http://127.0.0.1:11434
OPENCLAW_HOME: isolated temp dir
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH: isolated temp openclaw.json
OLLAMA_API_KEY: ollama-local
OLLAMA_TAGS_BEFORE: {"models":[{"name":"smollm2:135m","model":"smollm2:135m","modified_at":"2026-06-28T13:57:38.596605328Z","size":270898672,"digest":"9077fe9d2ae1a4a41a868836b56b8163731a8fe16621397028c2c76f838c6907","details":{"format":"gguf","family":"llama","families":["llama"],"parameter_size":"134.52M","quantization_level":"F16","context_length":8192,"embedding_length":576},"capabilities":["completion"]}]}
EXIT: 0
```

```json
{
  "count": 1,
  "models": [
    {
      "key": "ollama/smollm2:135m",
      "name": "smollm2:135m",
      "input": "text",
      "contextWindow": 8192,
      "local": true,
      "available": true,
      "tags": [],
      "missing": false
    }
  ]
}
```

- Supporting loopback proof: before the Dockerized daemon was available, the same OpenClaw CLI entrypoint was also run against a local Ollama-compatible endpoint and returned `available: true` for `ollama/qwen3.6:35b-a3b`.
- Not tested / proof gaps: I did not install native Windows Ollama or run against the user's normal local model store. The live proof above uses a real Ollama daemon running in Docker Desktop with a pulled local model and exercises OpenClaw's real CLI entrypoint, config loading, provider discovery HTTP calls, and JSON output.

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): gracefully handle unparsable YAML frontmatter in vault scans (#96125)
target.body:
Related to #96125.

## What Problem This Solves

A single wiki page with malformed YAML frontmatter (e.g., a `sourceIds` list item containing inline `:` inside bold markdown — `**MEMORY.md line 235**:"..."`) crashes `wiki_apply`, `wiki_lint`, and any vault-wide scan via `compileMemoryWikiVault`. The `YAMLParseError` propagates from `parseWikiMarkdown` → `toWikiPageSummary` → `readPageSummaries` (which uses `errorMode: "stop"`) → entire vault compilation fails, even though the offending page has already been written successfully.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix is placed in `toWikiPageSummary` — the scanning boundary — rather than `parseWikiMarkdown` — the parsing primitive:

- **Scanning callers** (`compile`, `lint`, `query`, `status`) all go through `toWikiPageSummary`. Catching here makes the entire vault resilient: one bad page produces a degraded summary with `frontmatterError` set, other pages compile normally.
- **Edit callers** (`apply`, `chatgpt-import`) call `parseWikiMarkdown` directly. They still throw on bad frontmatter so a write never silently overwrites existing metadata with empty fields.

If we caught in `parseWikiMarkdown` instead, edit paths would accept empty `{}` frontmatter → write back → **lose all metadata** (`id`, `sourceIds`, `claims`, etc.) on the page being edited. That is a data-loss bug.

The `splitWikiFrontmatterBlock` helper is extracted from `parseWikiMarkdown` so the catch block can still strip the frontmatter block from the raw content and preserve the body, even when YAML parsing fails.

## Root Cause

```text
parseWikiMarkdown()                                    markdown.ts:181
  → YAML.parse(match[1]) throws YAMLParseError         no try-catch
    → toWikiPageSummary() dies                         markdown.ts:620
      → readPageSummaries() errorMode:"stop" kills all read-with-concurrency.ts:62
        → compileMemoryWikiVault() throws              compile.ts:397-398
```

Introduced by: `1ee2733b2f` (vincentkoc, 2026-06-17) — initial memory-wiki extraction.  
The `YAML.parse` call has never had error handling since the module was introduced.  
Confidence: **clear**.

> **Note on existing PR**: PR #96177 identifies the same fix surface (catch in `toWikiPageSummary` + `frontmatterError` field + `invalid-frontmatter` lint). This PR adds a control-group regression test (valid YAML unaffected), an extra test assertion verifying the body survives degradation, and a more complete proof matrix produced with `node --import tsx` driving the real production `toWikiPageSummary`. The editorial call-path invariant (apply/chatgpt-import must still throw) is explicitly documented in the code comment.

## User Impact

- `wiki_apply`, `wiki_lint`, and any vault scan no longer fail because of an unrelated page elsewhere in the vault
- The broken page shows `invalid-frontmatter` in lint output with the exact parse error message, so users know which file to fix
- Other pages in the vault compile and lint normally

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: A wiki page with unparsable YAML frontmatter no longer crashes vault-wide scans. The degraded summary carries `frontmatterError` so lint reports the broken file. Valid pages are unaffected.

**Real environment tested**: Linux, Node v22.11.0, OpenClaw PR branch `fix/wiki-frontmatter-yaml-parse-96125` at `origin/main` = `68ead4dd80`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { toWikiPageSummary } from './extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.ts';
// ... (3 scenarios: bad frontmatter, good frontmatter, no frontmatter)
"
```

**Evidence after fix** — matrix of input variants vs observed results:

| Input | frontmatterError | hasFrontmatter | id | title |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `sourceIds` with inline `:` in bold (the #96125 case) | `"Unexpected scalar at node end at line 4, column 17: …"` | `true` | `undefined` | `"Test Title"` |
| Valid `sourceIds: [source.alpha]` | `undefined` | `true` | `"synthesis.healthy"` | `"Healthy Page"` |
| No frontmatter block at all | `undefined` | `false` | `undefined` | `"Plain Page"` |

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  9 passed (9)

$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)
```

Full memory-wiki CI-like shard:

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/
 Test Files  27 passed | 2 failed (29)
      Tests  169 passed | 4 failed (173)
```

The 4 failures are all `missing node:sqlite` (Node v22.11 < 22.19 requirement) in unrelated tests (`doctor-contract-api`, `source-sync-state`). Pre-existing infrastructure gap.

**Observed result after fix**: The bad-frontmatter page produces a degraded `WikiPageSummary` with `frontmatterError` set, the page body preserved under its original title, and frontmatter-derived fields (id, sourceIds, etc.) falling back to empty. `wiki_lint` reports `invalid-frontmatter` on the offending page. Healthy pages compile and lint exactly as before.

**What was not tested**:
- Live OpenClaw gateway with a real vault containing mixed healthy/broken pages — this would require a running gateway instance. The `toWikiPageSummary` function is exercised at unit-test level with 3 input variants covering the full matrix.
- `wiki_apply` / `wiki_lint` end-to-end via gateway — these go through `compileMemoryWikiVault` → `readPageSummaries` → `toWikiPageSummary`, which is the same call chain tested in the lint integration test.

## Regression Test Plan

```console
$ pnpm vitest run extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts
```

- `toWikiPageSummary` — degrades to empty frontmatter instead of throwing (#96125)
- `toWikiPageSummary` — preserves frontmatter and body for valid YAML (#96125 control)
- `lintMemoryWikiVault` — reports unparsable frontmatter as a lint issue (#96125)

## Changes

- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.ts`: +25/-3 — add `frontmatterError` field to `WikiPageSummary`, extract `splitWikiFrontmatterBlock` helper, catch `YAML.parse` in `toWikiPageSummary` with degraded fallback
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.ts`: +15/-0 — add `invalid-frontmatter` lint code, skip frontmatter-derived checks when `frontmatterError` is set
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/markdown.test.ts`: +62/-0 — 2 new tests: degraded parse preserves body, valid YAML unaffected
- `extensions/memory-wiki/src/lint.test.ts`: +41/-0 — integration test: broken page gets `invalid-frontmatter`, healthy page unaffected

target.title: test(plugins): cover filtered bundle MCP servers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR tightens the existing compatible-bundle MCP path for bundles that need a narrower default tool surface.

- Adds regression coverage that a Codex-compatible bundle `.mcp.json` preserves `toolFilter.include`, `toolFilter.exclude`, and a relative bundle data root.
- Documents bundle MCP tool filters on the plugin-bundles page alongside the existing transport and tool-naming guidance.
- Keeps scope limited to bundle MCP loading/docs; runtime filtering behavior already exists and is covered separately.

Reviewers should focus on whether the bundle MCP docs accurately describe the existing runtime behavior and whether the new compatible-bundle regression is useful.

## Linked context

No issue filed.

Related precedent investigated: bundle MCP support and follow-up coverage in prior OpenClaw PRs such as #48611, #71287, #79686, and #88399. This PR follows that existing bundle MCP seam.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Compatible bundles that contribute MCP servers can ship a narrow default tool surface through `toolFilter.include` and `toolFilter.exclude`; OpenClaw docs now describe that bundle path and a regression test proves the loader preserves the filter and relative app root.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `test/filtered-bundle-mcp`, commit `d5bffb3dc0`, with an isolated temporary `HOME`, `OPENCLAW_HOME`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Created a temporary Codex-compatible bundle containing `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, `.mcp.json`, `skills/`, and `apps/`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins install --link <temp>/vision-mcp`; ran `pnpm openclaw plugins inspect vision-mcp --json`; then loaded bundle MCP config with `node --import tsx` calling `loadEnabledBundleMcpConfig(...)` against the isolated home/config.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the temporary real OpenClaw setup:

```text
Proof root: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins install --link /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Linked plugin path: /var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp
Restart the gateway to load plugins.
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs plugins inspect vision-mcp --json
{
  "diagnostics": [],
  "command": "npx",
  "args": [
    "-y",
    "@vision-mcp/cli@latest",
    "serve",
    "--apps-root",
    "/private/var/folders/.../openclaw-bundle-proof.gE7Fjt/vision-mcp/apps"
  ],
  "cwdEndsWith": true,
  "toolFilter": {
    "include": [
      "capsule.*",
      "vision_map.snapshot",
      "vision_map.list_apps",
      "vision_map.list_workflows",
      "vision_map.describe_workflow",
      "vision_map.describe",
      "vision_map.detect_state",
      "vision_map.list_actions",
      "vision_map.describe_action",
      "vision_map.perform_action",
      "vision_map.run_workflow",
      "vision_map.verify",
      "vision_map.repair_minimal",
      "vision_map.export_trace"
    ],
    "exclude": [
      "vision_map.click_at",
      "vision_map.type_text",
      "vision_map.press_key",
      "vision_map.scroll",
      "vision_map.init",
      "vision_map.apply_patch",
      "vision_map.add_control",
      "vision_map.commit_*",
      "vision_map.harvest_session"
    ]
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw linked the temporary compatible bundle, detected its bundle MCP server, preserved both include and exclude filter arrays, set `cwd` to the bundle root, and resolved `./apps` to an absolute path inside the installed bundle.

What was not tested: I did not launch the MCP server process, connect to a live desktop MCP server, or perform GUI automation. I also did not run a full Gateway agent turn against the temporary bundle.

Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof uses a temporary local compatible bundle and the real OpenClaw plugin installer/loader, but the MCP server command itself is not started because this PR changes documentation and loader regression coverage, not MCP runtime execution behavior.

## Tests and validation

Commands run:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs -- src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts --reporter=verbose
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm check:docs
codex review --base origin/main
```

Regression coverage added: `src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts` now covers a Codex-compatible bundle MCP config with a filtered server and a relative `./apps` data root.

Validation results:

```text
src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: 1 file passed, 8 tests passed
pnpm format:check src/plugins/bundle-mcp.test.ts: All matched files use the correct format
git diff --check: passed
pnpm check:docs: Docs formatting clean; markdownlint 0 errors; MDX check passed; i18n glossary passed; checked_internal_links=4554; broken_links=0
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime behavior change; docs now describe existing bundle MCP filter behavior.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`): No runtime change. The docs encourage narrow bundle MCP defaults for high-risk tool surfaces.

What is the highest-risk area? The docs could overstate which configured MCP fields bundle MCP entries support.

How is that risk mitigated? Existing runtime code already reads `toolFilter` from bundle-provided server config, existing runtime tests cover filtering behavior, and this PR adds loader coverage proving the bundle file path preserves the filter before runtime discovery.

## Current review state

AI-assisted: yes.

Next action: maintainer review.

target.title: docs(memory): align descriptors and docs with recursive memory/**/*.md
target.body:
## Summary
- The runtime already walks `memory/` recursively via `collectMemoryFilesFromDir` (`packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`), so nested files like `memory/YYYY-MM/YYYY-MM-DD.md` are indexed and searched. But several agent-facing descriptors and public docs still said `memory/*.md`, which can mislead agents into thinking the index is flat.
- This PR updates the remaining stale references to `memory/**/*.md`. No runtime behavior change.

## Files touched
- `extensions/memory-core/index.ts` — lazy `memory_search` / `memory_get` descriptors
- `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts` — concrete `memory_search` / `memory_get` descriptions
- `extensions/memory-core/src/prompt-section.ts` — system-prompt recall guidance
- `src/config/schema.help.ts` — `agents.defaults.memorySearch` help text
- `docs/concepts/memory-builtin.md`, `memory.md`, `system-prompt.md`
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`, `docs/reference/token-use.md`
- `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` regenerated via `pnpm config:docs:gen`

## Note
This supersedes #72748. That PR also carried QMD watcher changes that were independently resolved upstream by #81802 (`watch-settle.ts`), so the surviving value was just this wording alignment. Splitting it out as a small, behavior-neutral PR for easier review.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Agent-facing descriptors and public docs said `memory/*.md` even though the runtime already walks `memory/` recursively and indexes nested files such as `memory/YYYY-MM/YYYY-MM-DD.md`. This PR aligns the wording to `memory/**/*.md` so agents and readers see what is actually indexed. The change is wording-only; the proof below shows (a) the installed runtime already exhibits the recursive behavior the new wording describes, and (b) the after-patch descriptor and prompt strings on this branch actually carry the new `memory/**/*.md` wording when imported at runtime.

**Real environment tested**: Installed OpenClaw `2026.5.20` (npm global, current `latest` published build, ahead of the `acf265d` base this PR branches from), against the live workspace `~/.openclaw/workspace-moku` used by the gateway. Agent `main`, local embedding provider, per-agent SQLite memory store at `~/.openclaw/memory/main.sqlite`. After-patch text proof imports the modified TypeScript modules directly from this branch via `node --import tsx` against working tree at HEAD `30f15f4e22`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

Step 1 — drop a nested dated memory file under `memory/2026-05/` with a unique token:

```
cat > ~/.openclaw/workspace-moku/memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md <<'EOF'
Recursive memory search proof.
Unique token for PR 85899 smoke test: PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY
This file lives at memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md
EOF
```

Step 2 — reindex the live agent's memory store:

```
openclaw memory index
```

Step 3 — search for the unique token to prove the nested path was indexed and is retrievable:

```
openclaw memory search "PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY" --max-results 3
```

Step 4 — on the PR branch checkout (`30f15f4e22`), import the modified descriptor/help modules at runtime via `tsx` and print the after-patch strings to prove the new `memory/**/*.md` wording is observable from a real runtime call site (not just the diff):

```
node --import tsx _proof-c.mjs   # imports FIELD_HELP and buildPromptSection from this branch
```

**After-fix evidence**: Terminal output from the installed `openclaw 2026.5.20` runtime on the live workspace, plus runtime-imported after-patch strings from this branch (absolute paths redacted to `~`):

```
$ openclaw memory status
Memory Search (main)
Provider: local (requested: local)
Sources: memory
Extra paths: ~/.openclaw/shared-memory
Indexed: 105/105 files . 933 chunks
Workspace: ~/.openclaw/workspace-moku
By source:
  memory . 105/105 files . 933 chunks
```

```
$ openclaw memory index
[node-llama-cpp] ... falling back to using no GPU
Memory index updated (main).
```

```
$ openclaw memory search "PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY" --max-results 3
[node-llama-cpp] ... falling back to using no GPU
0.812 memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md:1-9
    Recursive memory search proof.
    Unique token for PR 85899 smoke test: PROOFTOKEN_QXZ7K_NESTED_RECURSIVE_MEMORY
    This file lives at memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md
    to verify that nested dated memory files are indexed and searchable
    by the runtime.

0.450 memory/fix-openclaw/memory-recursive-search/smoke/2026-04-27.md:1-6
0.432 memory/fix-openclaw/memory-recursive-search/pr-draft.md:1-32
```

After-patch text observed from this branch (HEAD `30f15f4e22`) by importing the modified modules at runtime:

```
$ node --import tsx _proof-c.mjs
=== schema.help.ts: FIELD_HELP['agents.defaults.memorySearch'] ===
Vector search over MEMORY.md and memory/**/*.md (per-agent overrides supported).

=== prompt-section.ts: buildPromptSection({ memory_search + memory_get }) ===
    [section header line: "## Memory Recall"]
    Before answering anything about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos: run memory_search on MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md + indexed session transcripts; then use memory_get to pull only the needed lines. If low confidence after search, say you checked.
    Citations are disabled: do not mention file paths or line numbers in replies unless the user explicitly asks.

=== prompt-section.ts: buildPromptSection({ memory_search only }) ===
    [section header line: "## Memory Recall"]
    Before answering anything about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos: run memory_search on MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md + indexed session transcripts and answer from the matching results. If low confidence after search, say you checked.
    Citations are disabled: do not mention file paths or line numbers in replies unless the user explicitly asks.

=== extensions/memory-core/index.ts (post-patch source lines) ===
 127      description:
 128        "Mandatory recall step: semantically search MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md (and optional session transcripts) before answering questions about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos. ...",

 139      description:
 140        "Safe exact excerpt read from MEMORY.md or memory/**/*.md. Defaults to a bounded excerpt when lines are omitted, includes truncation/continuation info when more content exists, and `corpus=wiki` reads from registered compiled-wiki supplements.",

=== extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts (post-patch source lines) ===
 240      description:
 241        "Mandatory recall step: semantically search MEMORY.md + memory/**/*.md (and optional session transcripts) before answering questions about prior work, decisions, dates, people, preferences, or todos. ...",

 410      description:
 411        "Safe exact excerpt read from MEMORY.md or memory/**/*.md. Defaults to a bounded excerpt when lines are omitted, includes truncation/continuation info when more content exists, and `corpus=wiki` reads from registered compiled-wiki supplements.",
```

**Observed result after fix**: (a) The freshly written nested file `memory/2026-05/2026-05-24-recursive-proof.md` (two levels deep under `memory/`) was indexed by `openclaw memory index` and returned as the top match (score `0.812`) for the unique token. A second nested hit from `memory/fix-openclaw/memory-recursive-search/smoke/2026-04-27.md` (three levels deep) confirms recursion is not limited to one level. (b) Importing the modified `FIELD_HELP` map and `buildPromptSection` from this branch at runtime shows the after-patch strings carry `memory/**/*.md` for both the config help text and the system-prompt memory recall guidance, matching the diff exactly. (c) The two `memory_search` / `memory_get` description strings in `extensions/memory-core/index.ts` and `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts` are inline string literals; the after-patch lines printed from this branch show the new wording in place. No runtime code paths are modified, only the descriptor and doc strings plus the regenerated `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256`.

**What was not tested**: This PR does not introduce or modify any runtime code path, so additional behavior tests beyond the search smoke above were not added. The recursive walk itself (`collectMemoryFilesFromDir`) is already covered by upstream tests under `packages/memory-host-sdk/`.

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:gen` regenerates baseline cleanly (no other drift)
- [x] Nested memory smoke search on installed `2026.5.20` returns the new nested file at score `0.812`
- [x] On-branch runtime import shows `FIELD_HELP['agents.defaults.memorySearch']`, `buildPromptSection(...)` output, and `memory_search` / `memory_get` description literals all carry `memory/**/*.md`
- [ ] CI: lint / type / config-baseline check
